title: Wander: The Cult of Barnacle Bay - Let’s Play and Review - Part 5
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=D4p6nv2t2FA

Now above ground (and filmed on a shiny new camera), Daniel (@dungeondive) presses on with **Wander: The Cult of Barnacle Bay** — and this session surfaces his sharpest structural critique of the game. Because spawns are tied to hero XP and most objectives demand clearing *every* enemy, the party is effectively forced to keep leveling, which keeps spawning more foes, which drags the back half of each scenario out precisely when it should accelerate.

He frames it with a Kill Bill analogy: films use editing to make the climactic melee against dozens *shorter* than the earlier one-on-one duels; Wander does the opposite, piling on enemies so the finale becomes a long slog of rolling attack and defence dice. And this session proves the point painfully — swarmed by grunts and archers, Tank suffers a brutal run of terrible dice, fails block after block, and finally goes down. Daniel ends mid-fight, needing to read up on how to revive a downed hero.

It's an honest, unglamorous look at the game's low point: the moment the dice turn cold and the design's pacing works against you rather than with you.

----

Daniel loves Wander but admits its endgame bogs down exactly when it should climax. Do you forgive a pacing flaw in a game whose moment-to-moment play you enjoy, or does it sour the whole thing?
